metadata.subject: developer-operating-systems
metadata.topic: Advanced Scheduling: MLFQ, Proportional Share, Multiprocessor (Chapters 8, 9, 10)

questions.text: What is the Multi-level Feedback Queue (MLFQ) scheduler?

options.text: A scheduler that uses a single queue with equal priority for all jobs
A scheduler with multiple queues, each with different priority levels, and rules for job movement
A scheduler that only uses lottery-based ticket allocation
A scheduler that requires knowing job run times in advance

explanation.correct: MLFQ is a sophisticated scheduler that has a number of queues, each assigned a different priority level. Rules dictate how processes are handled in the queues, and it learns job behavior over time without needing to know run times in advance.

incorrect.A: This describes a single-queue approach, not MLFQ.
incorrect.C: This describes lottery scheduling, not MLFQ.
incorrect.D: MLFQ does NOT require knowing job run times in advance - that's the problem it solves.

questions.text: Which of the following are rules of MLFQ? (Select all that apply)

options.text: If Priority(A) > Priority(B), choose A
If Priority(A) = Priority(B), run both simultaneously
When a job enters the system, it gets highest priority
Once a job uses up its time allotment, its priority is reduced

explanation.correct: MLFQ Rules: 1) If Priority(A) > Priority(B), choose A. 2) If equal priority, run in Round Robin. 3) New jobs get highest priority. 4) After using time allotment, priority is reduced (move down a queue).

incorrect.B: Jobs with equal priority run in Round Robin (time-slicing), not simultaneously.

questions.text: What problem does the priority boost (Rule 5) solve in MLFQ?

options.text: It speeds up interactive jobs
It prevents starvation by periodically moving all jobs to the topmost queue
It eliminates the need for multiple queues
It reduces context switch overhead

explanation.correct: Priority boost (Rule 5) periodically moves all jobs in the system to the topmost (highest priority) queue. This prevents starvation by ensuring long-running jobs occasionally get a chance to run at high priority.

incorrect.A: Priority boost helps all jobs, not just interactive ones.
incorrect.C: Priority boost doesn't eliminate queues; it just resets job positions.
incorrect.D: Priority boost doesn't directly affect context switch overhead.

questions.text: What is starvation in the context of MLFQ?

options.text: When a process uses too much CPU time at once
When interactive jobs consume all CPU time, leaving long jobs never running
When the priority boost fails to work
When context switching overhead becomes too high

explanation.correct: Starvation in MLFQ occurs when there are too many interactive jobs (which maintain high priority due to I/O) that consume all CPU time, causing long-running jobs to never receive CPU time as they keep getting demoted.

incorrect.A: This describes a CPU-bound process, not starvation.
incorrect.C: Priority boost is a solution to starvation, not starvation itself.
incorrect.D: This is context switch overhead, not starvation.

questions.text: What is Proportional Share scheduling?

options.text: A technique that gives equal time slices to all processes
A technique that prioritizes an equal amount of CPU time for each process based on tickets
A technique that always runs the shortest job first
A technique that uses a single queue for all processes

explanation.correct: Proportional Share is a scheduling technique that prioritizes processes based on the number of tickets they hold, which represents their share of system resources (CPU time).

incorrect.A: Equal time slices describes Round Robin, not Proportional Share.
incorrect.C: This describes Shortest Job First, not Proportional Share.
incorrect.D: Proportional Share can use various queue structures.

questions.text: Which of the following are types of Proportional Share schedulers? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Lottery Scheduler
Stride Scheduler
Multi-level Feedback Queue
Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)

explanation.correct: Types of Proportional Share schedulers include: 1) Lottery Scheduler, 2) Stride Scheduler, and 3) Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS). MLFQ is a different type of scheduler.

incorrect.C: MLFQ is not a Proportional Share scheduler; it's a priority-based scheduler.

questions.text: How does the Lottery Scheduler work?

options.text: It uses a deterministic approach to give exact CPU proportions
It uses randomness: tickets represent CPU share, and winners are chosen randomly
It always runs the process with the most tickets first
It uses a fixed time slice for all processes

explanation.correct: The Lottery Scheduler uses randomness: the number of tickets a process has represents its share of CPU time. Winners are chosen randomly, achieving proportions probabilistically over time.

incorrect.A: This describes Stride Scheduler, which is deterministic.
incorrect.C: Lottery doesn't always run the highest-ticket process; it's random.
incorrect.D: Lottery doesn't use fixed time slices like Round Robin.

questions.text: What is a limitation of the Lottery Scheduler?

options.text: It is too complex to implement
It occasionally will not deliver the exact desired proportions of CPU time
It requires knowing job run times in advance
It cannot handle multiple processes

explanation.correct: The use of randomness in lottery scheduling leads to probabilistic correctness in meeting desired proportions, but there's no guarantee of exact proportions. For example, a process with 25% tickets might only get 20% CPU time.

incorrect.A: Lottery Scheduler is actually lightweight and simple to implement.
incorrect.C: It doesn't require knowing run times in advance.
incorrect.D: It handles multiple processes well using tickets.

questions.text: How does the Stride Scheduler differ from the Lottery Scheduler?

options.text: Stride is probabilistic, Lottery is deterministic
Stride is deterministic and gets proportions exactly right, Lottery is probabilistic
Stride uses time slices, Lottery does not
Stride requires knowing run times, Lottery does not

explanation.correct: Stride Scheduler is deterministic - it gets proportions exactly right at the end of each scheduling cycle. Lottery Scheduler achieves proportions probabilistically over time. Stride uses a 'stride' value (inverse of tickets) to determine scheduling order.

incorrect.A: This is backwards - Stride is deterministic, Lottery is probabilistic.
incorrect.C: Neither uses traditional time slices like Round Robin.
incorrect.D: Both use tickets; stride calculates stride from tickets.

questions.text: What is the Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)?

options.text: A scheduler that uses fixed time slices for all processes
A scheduler that uses virtual runtime (vruntime) to fairly divide CPU among processes
A scheduler that only runs the shortest jobs
A scheduler that uses lottery tickets

explanation.correct: CFS (used in Linux) aims to fairly divide CPU among competing processes using virtual runtime (vruntime). Each process's vruntime increases at the same rate, and CFS picks the process with the lowest vruntime to run next.

incorrect.A: CFS doesn't use fixed time slices; it uses dynamic scheduling based on vruntime.
incorrect.C: This describes Shortest Job First, not CFS.
incorrect.D: This describes Lottery Scheduler, not CFS.

questions.text: How does CFS determine when to switch processes?

options.text: sched_latency: determines how often to switch (like a dynamic time slice)
min_granularity: minimum time slice if too many processes exist
niceness: weighting to give certain processes higher CPU share
time_slice: fixed time slice for all processes

explanation.correct: CFS uses: 1) sched_latency - determines switching frequency (dynamic time slice = sched_latency / number_of_processes), 2) min_granularity - minimum time slice if too many processes, and 3) niceness - weighting for priority.

incorrect.D: CFS doesn't use a fixed time_slice for all processes.

questions.text: What data structure does CFS use to organize running processes?

options.text: Array
Linked List
Red-Black Tree (organized by vruntime)
Hash Table

explanation.correct: CFS organizes running processes using a Red-Black Tree data structure, with processes organized by vruntime. This allows quick lookups and operations in logarithmic time.

incorrect.A: Arrays don't provide efficient lookups for this purpose.
incorrect.B: Linked lists would be too slow for the required operations.
incorrect.D: Hash tables aren't suitable for ordered operations needed by CFS.

questions.text: What is Single Queue Multiprocessor Scheduling (SQMS)?

options.text: Each processor has its own scheduling queue
All processors share a single, global ready queue
Processes are scheduled using lottery tickets
Processes are scheduled by priority only

explanation.correct: Single Queue Multiprocessor Scheduling (SQMS) uses a single, global ready queue that all processors share. It's simple but has scalability issues.

incorrect.D: Priority scheduling is a different concept.

questions.text: What are shortcomings of Single Queue Multiprocessor Scheduling? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Requires locking (mutex) to prevent collisions
Doesn't scale - locking reduces performance as CPU count increases
Cannot utilize cache efficiently (less cache affinity)
Cannot handle more than one process at a time

explanation.correct: SQMS shortcomings: 1) Requires locking for the shared queue, 2) Doesn't scale - locking overhead increases with CPU count, and 3) Can't utilize cache efficiently because processes move across CPUs (poor cache affinity).

incorrect.D: SQMS can handle multiple processes; the queue holds many processes.

questions.text: What is Multi-Queue Multiprocessor Scheduling (MQMS)?

options.text: A single queue shared by all processors
Each processor has its own queue, jobs are scheduled independently
A scheduling method that only uses lottery tickets
A method that requires all jobs to have equal priority

explanation.correct: Multi-Queue Multiprocessor Scheduling (MQMS) gives each processor its own scheduling queue. Jobs are placed on one queue (based on heuristic) and scheduled essentially independently, avoiding locking and improving cache affinity.

incorrect.A: This describes Single Queue Multiprocessor Scheduling.
incorrect.D: MQMS doesn't require equal priority for all jobs.

questions.text: What advantage does MQMS have over SQMS?

options.text: It is simpler to implement
It avoids locking and provides better cache affinity
It guarantees perfect load balancing
It requires less memory

explanation.correct: MQMS advantages over SQMS: 1) As CPU count grows, so do queues, so lock/cache contention isn't a central problem, and 2) It intrinsically provides cache affinity since jobs stay on the same CPU.

incorrect.A: MQMS is more complex due to the need for load balancing.
incorrect.C: MQMS can suffer from load imbalance - it does NOT guarantee perfect balancing.
incorrect.D: MQMS typically uses more memory (multiple queues).

questions.text: What is a disadvantage of Multi-Queue Multiprocessor Scheduling?

options.text: It requires too much locking
It can suffer from load imbalance between queues
It cannot handle multiple processors
It has poor cache affinity

explanation.correct: A disadvantage of MQMS is load imbalance - one queue might have many jobs while another has few, leading to unequal CPU time distribution. This requires job migration (work stealing) to fix.

incorrect.A: MQMS avoids locking by having separate queues.
incorrect.C: MQMS is designed specifically for multiple processors.
incorrect.D: MQMS actually has good cache affinity.

questions.text: What is work stealing in MQMS?

options.text: A technique where jobs are stolen from other processes' memory
A technique where a queue low on jobs steals from a fuller queue to balance load
A technique that always keeps all queues equal
A technique that eliminates the need for scheduling

explanation.correct: Work stealing is a solution to load imbalance in MQMS. A queue that is low on jobs will occasionally peek at another queue, and if it is more full, it will steal a job to balance the load.

incorrect.A: Work stealing is about balancing job queues, not stealing from processes' memory.
incorrect.C: Work stealing doesn't guarantee all queues stay equal; it's periodic rebalancing.
incorrect.D: Work stealing is a scheduling enhancement, not a replacement.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about MLFQ and CFS are true? (Select all that apply)

options.text: MLFQ uses priority queues, CFS uses a Red-Black Tree
MLFQ requires knowing run times in advance, CFS does not
Both MLFQ and CFS can handle interactive and CPU-bound processes
CFS uses vruntime to determine which process runs next

explanation.correct: A) MLFQ uses priority queues, CFS uses Red-Black Tree. C) Both can handle interactive and CPU-bound processes. D) CFS uses vruntime (lowest vruntime runs next). B is false - MLFQ does NOT require knowing run times in advance.

incorrect.B: MLFQ was specifically designed to NOT require knowing run times in advance.

questions.text: What is the relationship between Proportional Share scheduling and multiprocessor scheduling?

options.text: Proportional Share is only used in single-processor systems
Multiprocessor scheduling (SQMS/MQMS) can use Proportional Share algorithms like CFS at each queue
Proportional Share replaces the need for multiprocessor scheduling
Multiprocessor scheduling only uses priority-based approaches

explanation.correct: Multiprocessor scheduling (SQMS/MQMS) can use various scheduling algorithms at each queue, including Proportional Share algorithms like CFS. The queue structure and the algorithm used within each queue are separate concerns.

incorrect.A: Proportional Share can be used in multiprocessor systems.
incorrect.C: Proportional Share is a CPU allocation method, not a replacement for multiprocessor queue management.
incorrect.D: Multiprocessor scheduling can use any algorithm, including Proportional Share.
